title: The Pilot (Part 1)

jokes.text: Hi. My name is Bill. And the one thing I'm worried about is having a stereo and a cream-colored couch.
jokes.setup: Fear of success concept leading to AA-type meetings
jokes.punchline: Mundane material possessions as the feared 'success'
jokes.explanation: Sharp observational humor that takes the abstract concept of 'fear of success' and makes it absurdly concrete with specific, mundane items. Classic Seinfeld structure.

jokes.text: Now, this means, to the average person, if you have to go to a funeral, you're better off in the casket than doing the eulogy.
jokes.setup: People fear public speaking more than death
jokes.punchline: Logical conclusion: better to be dead than give eulogy
jokes.explanation: Brilliant logical construction that takes a statistic and draws an absurd but mathematically sound conclusion. Perfect setup-punchline structure with dark humor twist.

jokes.text: Why do we need two people in the show that can't act?
jokes.setup: Kramer wants to play himself in the pilot
jokes.punchline: Jerry admits he can't act either
jokes.explanation: Self-aware meta humor that acknowledges the show's own casting reality. Good character integration and economy.

jokes.text: My grandmother's in the hospital. / Your grandmother's in the hospital!
jokes.setup: Kramer trying to demonstrate acting ability
jokes.punchline: Jerry's over-the-top fake reaction
jokes.explanation: Solid character moment showing both characters' inability to act, but fairly straightforward execution.

jokes.text: I've never been to Mars, but I imagine it's quite lovely.
jokes.setup: Jerry attempting to say something funny for Kramer to react to
jokes.punchline: Mundane observation about Mars delivered deadpan
jokes.explanation: Absurdist humor with good deadpan delivery, treating the extraordinary as ordinary.

jokes.text: Mine was better than that!
jokes.setup: Jerry's attempt at humor about Mars
jokes.punchline: Kramer's indignant comparison to his hospital line
jokes.explanation: Simple callback that works for Kramer's character but isn't particularly clever.

jokes.text: Do you, uh...have a ketchup secret?
jokes.setup: Russell desperately trying to connect with Elaine
jokes.punchline: Random, nonsensical question about ketchup
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Russell's awkward desperation with an absurd non sequitur. Great character moment.

jokes.text: I don't know why-- Why don't they just put pretzels out on the table? Or even peanuts would be good. But I don't know who eats these cheesy things.
jokes.setup: Russell pouring his heart out to Elaine
jokes.punchline: Elaine completely ignoring him to complain about snack mix
jokes.explanation: Great cringe comedy - the complete mismatch between Russell's emotional state and Elaine's mundane concerns.

jokes.text: He's that, um, 'Did you ever notice this? Did you ever notice that?' guy.
jokes.setup: Russell mentioning Jerry Seinfeld
jokes.punchline: Elaine reducing Jerry's comedy to a formulaic catchphrase
jokes.explanation: Meta-humor that parodies observational comedy tropes while being part of an observational comedy show.

jokes.text: It's about nothing. / What do you mean it's about nothing? / Well, it's-- It's really-- It's very unusual. / For example, what did you do today? / Um...I got up, um, I went to work, then I came here. / There's a show. That's a show.
jokes.setup: Russell explaining the pilot concept
jokes.punchline: Elaine's mundane day becomes TV content
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-commentary on the show's own premise, demonstrating the concept while explaining it.

jokes.text: You're telling the world, 'I give up!' 'I can't compete in normal society. I'm miserable, so I might as well be comfortable.'
jokes.setup: Jerry seeing Kramer in sweatpants
jokes.punchline: Elaborate psychological analysis of sweatpants
jokes.explanation: Classic Seinfeld observational humor that takes a simple clothing choice and finds deep social meaning.

jokes.text: God would never let me be successful. He'd kill me first. He'll never let me be happy. / I thought you didn't believe in God. / I do for the bad things.
jokes.setup: George's fear of success and belief in God
jokes.punchline: Only believes in God for punishment purposes
jokes.explanation: Perfect George logic - selective belief system based on pessimism. Excellent character integration and structure.

jokes.text: What kind of a therapist are you? I'm paying you, I'm scared that something terrible's gonna happen to me, right away, you start looking for tumors?
jokes.setup: Therapist notices white spot on George's lip
jokes.punchline: George accuses therapist of being sadistic
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing George's paranoia and ability to blame others for his problems.

jokes.text: I bet you're rooting for a tumor.
jokes.setup: George's escalating anger at therapist
jokes.punchline: Accusing therapist of wanting him to have cancer
jokes.explanation: Dark humor with perfect economy. Captures George's paranoid worldview perfectly.

jokes.text: If I don't go to the doctor, nothing will happen. If I go, he might find something.
jokes.setup: Jerry suggesting George see a doctor
jokes.punchline: George's magical thinking about avoiding medical care
jokes.explanation: Relatable neurotic logic delivered with perfect George characterization.

jokes.text: Oh, my God, it's Crazy Joe Davola!
jokes.setup: Squeegee man approaching car
jokes.punchline: It's the recurring antagonist character
jokes.explanation: Good callback to recurring character creates tension and humor through recognition.

jokes.text: Good luck on the pilot, Jerry.
jokes.setup: Threatening character Joe Davola
jokes.punchline: Polite well-wishes delivered menacingly
jokes.explanation: Effectively creepy subversion of normal pleasantries. Good character work.

jokes.text: Anyone call for Vandelay Industries? / No, why? / Listen to me. I told the unemployment office I was close to a job with Vandelay Industries and I gave them your phone number. So when you answer the phone now, you've got to say, 'Vandelay Industries.' / Right! / I'm Vandelay Industries? / You're in latex.
jokes.setup: George's fake company scheme
jokes.punchline: Jerry must pretend to be a latex manufacturer
jokes.explanation: Classic Seinfeld running gag with good absurdist escalation. 'You're in latex' is perfectly delivered.

jokes.text: I just came from the podiatrist. I got something wrong with my foot. I got a little gangrene. They're probably gonna have to amputate.
jokes.setup: Actor auditioning for George role
jokes.punchline: Casually mentions upcoming amputation
jokes.explanation: Dark humor through casual delivery of serious medical condition. Works for the cringe factor.

jokes.text: A man gave me a...massage. / So? / Uh, well, he, uh... He had his hands, you know, and, uh, he was, uh... / He was what? / Well, he was, you know... touching and rubbing. / That's a massage. / I think it moved.
jokes.setup: George's discomfort about male massage therapist
jokes.punchline: His body's involuntary response
jokes.explanation: Classic George neurosis about masculinity and physical contact. Good build-up to awkward punchline.

jokes.text: It's like a bald convention out there!
jokes.setup: Actress entering audition room
jokes.punchline: Commenting on all the bald actors
jokes.explanation: Simple observational humor that creates awkward moment. Efficient but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: No, you didn't. He knows he's bald.
jokes.setup: Melissa's faux pas comment about baldness
jokes.punchline: Jerry's matter-of-fact response about George
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan delivery that defuses awkwardness while being funny about George's self-awareness.

jokes.text: What was that look? / What look? / That look you just gave me. / I gave a look? Yes.
jokes.setup: Actors reading dialogue about looks
jokes.punchline: The meta-commentary on acting and expressions
jokes.explanation: Meta-humor about acting and auditions, but not particularly strong execution.

jokes.text: I'm getting rid of all my furniture. All of it. I'm building levels... with steps. Completely carpeted... with pillows. Like ancient Egypt.
jokes.setup: Actor auditioning as Kramer explaining furniture plan
jokes.punchline: Bizarre ancient Egypt home design concept
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Kramer's absurd home improvement schemes. Great escalation from practical to completely bizarre.

jokes.text: Oh...I'll be comfortable.
jokes.setup: Question about comfort on his bizarre furniture setup
jokes.punchline: Confident understatement about ancient Egyptian living
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kramer-esque confidence in absurd living arrangements. Good economy and character work.

jokes.text: What happened to the raisins? Yeah, there was a box of raisins there. Did he just steal the raisins? Do you think he stole them?
jokes.setup: Actor leaving audition room
jokes.punchline: Noticing missing raisins and assuming theft
jokes.explanation: Absurdist humor about petty theft concerns. Classic Seinfeld focus on trivial details with serious discussion.

jokes.text: This is Martin Van Nostrand. / What are you doing here? / You two know each other? / Wait a minute. I know you! You're the guy from the Calvin Klein underwear ads.
jokes.setup: Kramer using fake name at audition
jokes.punchline: Recognition of the actor from underwear ads
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Kramer's fake identities combined with recognition humor. Solid but not exceptional.

tmdb_overview: Kramer seeks audition for Jerry and George's pilot; network president develops obsession with Elaine.
